title: Moong Dal Halwa

ingredients.name: split yellow moong dal, washed
visual_checks: soak in cold water for at least 4 hours or overnight before using
ingredients.notes: soak in cold water for at least 4 hours or overnight before using

subs.name: chana dal (Bengal gram), soaked identically — the halwa will be slightly denser with a more pronounced chickpea flavour

ingredients.name: ghee
visual_checks: do not skimp — it is what cooks the lentils
ingredients.notes: do not skimp — it is what cooks the lentils

subs.name: the recipe fundamentally requires ghee for both cooking and flavour; butter can work in a pinch
a pinch of turmeric for colour only, or simply omit

steps.text: Drain the soaked moong dal and grind it in a mixie with just 1–2 tbsp of water to a coarse paste that resembles wet sand — not a smooth slurry, and not chunky. The coarse texture is what gives the halwa its grainy character. Keep nearby.
Combine sugar and water in a saucepan, stir to dissolve, and bring to a gentle boil. Cook for 3–4 minutes to form a thin syrup. Steep the saffron in warm milk and set aside.

Melt the ghee in a wide, thick-bottomed kadai or iron pan over medium flame. Add the moong dal paste — it will splatter and hiss dramatically, so stand back briefly. Lower to medium-low and stir immediately and continuously with a long-handled spoon. The paste will stick together at first; keep pressing and folding.

Stir without stopping for 25–30 minutes. The paste will gradually lose moisture, begin to separate into granules, turn from pale yellow to golden-amber and smell intensely nutty and fragrant — like roasted chickpea crossed with caramel. This is the most critical stage: under-cooking at this point gives a gummy, raw-tasting halwa. The mixture is ready when the granules are uniform, golden and flow freely in the pan without sticking.
Pour the warm milk in a thin stream over the dal while stirring constantly — it will hiss and steam. Stir vigorously for 3–4 minutes until the milk is fully absorbed.

Pour the sugar syrup over the halwa and stir over medium-low heat for 8–10 minutes until the syrup is absorbed, the halwa is glossy, and ghee pools at the edges. Stir in saffron milk, cardamom, and the fried cashews and raisins (fry briefly in 1 tsp ghee). Serve hot — moong dal halwa sets firm when cold; reheat with a splash of milk.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: moong-dal → chana dal (Bengal gram), soaked identically — the halwa will be slightly denser with a more pronounced chickpea flavour; ghee → the recipe fundamentally requires ghee for both cooking and flavour; butter can work in a pinch; saffron → a pinch of turmeric for colour only, or simply omit
